Chapter 3
Alkanes and Cycloalkanes: Conformations and cis-trans Stereoisomers
© Steve Hamblin/ Alamy
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Section 3.1
CONFORMATIONAL ANALYSIS OF ETHANE

Conformation
The reactivity and properties of a compound depend to some extent on the shape of its molecules
The shape of a molecule with respect to bond rotations and other “easy” processes is called its conformation
We will study the energies and structures of conformations of alkanes and cycloalkanes
Three conformations of hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 )are shown. Conformation C, with an H – O–O – H angle of 115 degree, is the most stable .

Conformations of Ethane
Ethane (H 3 CCH 3 ) an infinite number of concentrations related by rotation about the C–C bond
The two most important are the staggered and eclipsed conformations
Staggered conformation: each C–H bond bisects an H–C–H
Eclipsed conformation: each C–H bond is aligned with an adjacent C–H bond
Conformations of Ethane-1

Torsion or Dihedral Angles
We are often interested in the angle between two bonds in a conformation (for example, the angle between adjacent C–H bonds in ethane)
Such an angle is called a torsion or dihedral angle
Certain torsion angles have special names.
Eclipsed = 0 degree, gauche = 60 degree, anti = 180 degree

Potential Energy and Conformation
Eclipsed conformers are least stable ; staggered conformers are most stable .

Potential Energy and Conformation-1
Energy differences are relatively small
Nonetheless, the vast majority of molecules are in a staggered conformation at any given time
The rotation process is extremely rapid ( t 1/2 ≈ 10 −6 s )
Increased energy of eclipsed conformer due to two factors:
Destabilization of eclipsed conformer due to nonbonding (steric) interactions
Stabilization of staggered conformer due to hyperconjugative σ C − H → σ* C−H interactions

Section 3.2
CONFORMATIONAL ANALYSIS OF BUTANE

Conformations of Butane
Butane (C 4 H 10 ) has two distinct staggered conformations, unlike ethane
The terminal CH 3 groups can be gauche …
Steric interaction between the gauche methyl groups is called a gauche interaction
…or anti
In the anti conformer, the CH 3 groups do not engage in steric interactions with one another

Potential Energy and Conformation-2
Note the impact of the gauche interaction on the gauche conformer!

Potential Energy and Conformation-3
Staggered conformers are still energy minima and eclipsed conformers are still maxima
The anti conformer is lower in energy than the gauche conformer due to a destabilizing steric interaction in the latter
Eclipsing CH 3 groups cause greater destabilization than H-H or H–CH 3 eclipsing

Section 3.3
CONFORMATIONS OF HIGHER ALKANES

Conformers of Larger Alkanes
Conformational analysis of larger alkanes quickly becomes complex
In general, the all- anti conformer is most stable
Viewed from the side, this conformer has a “zig-zag” appearance
This is why we draw alkyl chains as zig-zags !

Section 3.4
THE SHAPES OF CYCLOALKANES: PLANAR OR NONPLANAR?

Planar Cycloalkanes: A Prediction
The most stable cycloalkanes should have bond angles near 109.5 degree
If cycloalkanes are planar, cyclopentane ( 108 degree ) should be most stable, followed by cyclohexane ( 120 degree )
We can measure stability using enthalpies of combustion

Heats of Combustion of Cycloalkanes
Cyclopentane is not the most stable—cyclohexane is!
Heat per CH 2 combusted is smallest for cyclohexane
TABLE 3.1 Heat of combustion (− ∆ H ° ) of Cycloalkanes
Cycloalkane | Number of CH2 groups | Heat of combustion kJ/mol | Heat of combustion kcal/mol | Heat of combustion per CH2 group
kJ/mol | Heat of combustion per CH2 group
kcal/mol
Cyclopropane | 3 | 2,091 | 499.8 | 697 | 166.6
Cyclobutane | 4 | 2,745 | 656.1 | 686 | 164.0
Cyclopentane | 5 | 3,320 | 793.4 | 664 | 158.7
Cyclohexane | 6 | 3,953 | 944.7 | 659 | 157.5
Cycloheptane | 7 | 4,637 | 1108.4 | 662 | 158.3
Cyclooctane | 8 | 5,310 | 1269.2 | 664 | 158.7
Cyclononane | 9 | 5,981 | 1429.6 | 665 | 158.8
Cyclodecane | 10 | 6,639 | 1586.8 | 664 | 158.7
Cycloundecane | 11 | 7,293 | 1743.2 | 663 | 158.5
Cyclododecane | 12 | 7,922 | 1893.4 | 660 | 157.8
Cyclotetradecane | 14 | 9,234 | 2207.0 | 660 | 157.6
Cyclohexadecane | 16 | 10,548 | 2521.0 | 659 | 157.6
This is good evidence that cycloalkanes are not planar (with the exception of cyclopropane ).

Section 3.5
SMALL RINGS: CYCLOPROPANE AND CYCLOBUTANE

Cyclopropane
Cyclopropane is characterized by extremely severe angle strain —its internal C–C–C bond angle is only 60 degree
The C–C bonds in cyclopropane are bent and derived from the angled overlap of sp 3 hybrids
The internal C–C bonds are weak and liable to break
Cyclopropane also suffers from torsional strain due to eclipsing C–H’s

Cyclobutane
Angle strain in cyclobutane is not quite as bad as cyclopropane ( 90 degree versus 60 degree )
In addition, the cyclobutane ring “puckers” to minimize torsional or eclipsing strain
Nonetheless, cyclobutane is still somewhat unstable
